danger_signals.visual_form: Animated ghost sprites in various forms
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat requiring player action; touching ghost causes Ghostbuster to fall

danger_signals.visual_form: Large, distinctive sprite (e.g., Stay Puft Marshmallow Man variant)
danger_signals.meaning: Major threat; requires capture after defeat to progress
Environmental hazard when two Ghostbusters cross their positron streams
High-danger zone with random door encounters and ghost hazards

safety_signals.meaning: Restores 1 hit point; grants temporary durability increase

safety_signals.visual_form: Three-part icon resembling Tokuma Soft logo
safety_signals.meaning: Restores 3 hit points; more valuable health restoration

safety_signals.visual_form: Shop Workss (Japan) or shop environment between levels
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone for purchasing equipment, weapons, and supplies

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Shop Workss (Japan version) or generic shop (US version); Zuul Building as primary objective location

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Standard ghosts, Slimer (middle ghost enemy), Stay Puft Marshmallow Man (boss), other distinctive boss forms
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Haunted residential buildings, medieval castle level, interior of Zuul Building

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Presence of visible ghost sprites throughout levels; environmental corruption suggested by haunted building theme
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ghosts patrol/appear in random door encounters; player must eliminate them to progress

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Zuul Building presents as final, elevated objective; distinct interior aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must collect stone tablet pieces from four preliminary levels before accessing Zuul; requires boss defeats

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Urban decay suggested through level design; multiple building locations available for gameplay
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player-driven containment mission; economic system (money collection) tied to ghost elimination
Money earned from ghost elimination enables weapon upgrades, shields, and health items; progression gated by resource acquisition

environmental_storytelling.example: Four interchangeable haunted residential building levels establish ghost crisis scope; medieval castle level (unlocked after others) suggests escalating supernatural threat
Zuul Building serves as narrative focal point; stone tablet collection from four locations creates explicit goal structure communicated through level design
Standard ghosts populate early sections; Slimer appears as 'middle ghost'; unique bosses (Stay Puft, etc.) cap each level—establishes threat hierarchy through visual distinctiveness
Shop Workss (Japan version) reveals game developer as in-world equipment supplier—breaks fourth wall visually to explain resource availability
Zuul Building interior (where touching ghosts causes falling) contrasts with exterior streets (where ghost contact is harmless)—communicates state-dependent mechanics through environment

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Can crouch, jump, shoot positron gun in all directions; must capture defeated bosses
Falls upon ghost contact (distinct from exterior behavior); suggests weakened state or environmental hazard
Patrols levels; appears from random doors in Zuul Building; requires elimination to progress
Distinguishable sprite; appears at level midpoint or end; must be captured after defeat
Creates explosion effect (visual malfunction); however, no actual damage—likely unintended mechanic

notes: The NES Ghostbusters lacks sophisticated faction communication beyond basic hero/enemy opposition. World-state communication relies heavily on level design (haunted buildings, castle, Zuul Building) and numerical progression (stone tablet collection, money system) rather than visual manifestation of political or ideological conflict. The game's poor critical reception stemmed partly from weak presentation—limited visual variety in backgrounds, awkward Ghostbuster sprite design, and unclear mechanical feedback. Regional differences (Japan vs. US) suggest localization rather than thematic variation. Unused graphics (happy ghost sprites, flag icon) indicate cut content that might have added world-state nuance. The game world is primarily communicated through: (1) threat presence (ghost sprites), (2) resource scarcity (money, health items), (3) objective architecture (Zuul as focal point), and (4) level progression gating (tablet pieces as gate-keepers). No faction-specific visual language beyond the generic equipment/scientist vs. supernatural opposition.
